content.assessment: This proposal explicitly preserves the selection age and track structure, addressing only compensatory funding, teacher quality, and curriculum/instructional-time levers. From the Finnish comprehensive-school lens, this represents the inverse of the structural approach Finland took: rather than removing the early selection point, it attempts to offset its downstream effects. The cross-national tracking evidence indicates that early-tracking systems are associated with higher inequality than later-tracking systems independent of resourcing (F-live-finnish_reform-05), suggesting compensatory funding without structural change addresses a symptom rather than the identified driver. Additionally, Finland's own experience shows persistent within-school and within-system sorting mechanisms (emphasized classes, choice-driven segregation) even in a comprehensive structure with presumably higher average resourcing equality (F-live-finnish_reform-08, F-live-finnish_reform-09), implying that compensatory measures targeting resource gaps may not neutralize sorting-driven inequality that operates through mechanisms beyond funding.
opportunities: Could function as a complement to structural reform (as in t3) rather than a substitute, addressing resourcing gaps that would otherwise persist even after a tracking-age delay
strengths: Addressing instructional time and teacher-quality gaps is consistent with a general resourcing-equity logic, though the lens evidence supplied here speaks primarily to structural tracking effects rather than resourcing mechanisms specifically
threats: Because the lens finds that early tracking's inequality association appears independent of an equity-efficiency trade-off (F-live-finnish_reform-05), compensatory funding without structural change risks being a permanently partial remedy, requiring indefinite recurring investment to offset a structural feature that a Finland-style comprehensive reform would address more durably
weaknesses: Retains the age-10/12 selection point the lens identifies as the primary structural driver of the inequality gap in cross-national comparison (F-live-finnish_reform-05), so this proposal does not engage the mechanism most central to the lens's explanatory framework

Finland's persistent within-school sorting (emphasized classes) despite comprehensive, non-tracked structure (F-live-finnish_reform-08) suggests that compensatory resourcing alone, without structural or anti-sorting measures, is unlikely to fully counteract sorting dynamics, since sorting can operate through channels other than funding disparities
